summary_text: Scoring scale: `1` = low, `5` = high. For `Kernel fit`, higher is better. For `implementation cost` and `operational risk`, lower is better. | Option | Kernel fit | Implementation cost | Operational risk | Verdict | |---|---:|---:|---:|---| | Sidecar memory service | 5 | 3 | 2 | Best default | | Thin command shim | 4 | 1 | 1 | Best if you want minimal change | | Deep integration | 2 | 5 | 5 | Avoid | The reason is structural: Kernel’s contract already separates orchestration from runtime substrate and state truth, while ByteRover is explicitly daemon-first, MCP-enabled, and memory-centric ([Kernel sovereign adapter contract](/Users/masayuki/Dev/fugue-orchestrator-public/docs/kernel-sovereign-adapter-contract.md#L5), [Kernel runtime health](/Users/masayuki/Dev/fugue-orchestrator-public/scripts/lib/kernel-runtime-health.sh#L178), [ByteRover CLI reference](https://docs.byterover.dev/reference/cli-reference), [ByteRover README](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/campfirein/cipher/main/README.md)). That makes a sidecar or shim a clean fit, but deep integration would blur control-plane ownership and create a second source of truth. If you want the practical choice: use a thin command shim first if the goal is fast adoption, then evolve to a sidecar memory service if the memory surface proves valuable.
